q:
You drive in the Netherlands by car.

A. You follow the speed rules of that country.
B. You follow the Belgian rules.
e: Each country has its own rules.

You drive in the Netherlands by car.
A. You follow the speed rules of that country.
B. You follow the Belgian rules.
clean_e: Each country has its own rules.

q:
Beyond this sign ...

A. ... you may park left or right in parking spaces on the roadway.
B. ... the maximum permitted speed is 30 km / hour.
C. ... only pedestrians and cyclists are allowed.
e:
LESSON 6 - 'Special places'.
In a residential area (max 20 km / hour) cars can park both left and right on the parking spaces.

q:
How much is the maximum permitted speed in a 'school street'?
(Dutch: 'Schoolstraat' -- French: 'Rue scolaire').

A. Step feet.
B. 20 km / hour.
C. 30 km / hour.
e:
LESSON 6 - 'Special places'.
Vehicles that are allowed to drive in a school street must do this step-by-step.
(except if there are traffic signs that allow a different speed).

q:
What is wrong?
In a residential area or estate …

A. ... children are allowed to play on the road.
B. ... you are allowed to drive maximum 25 km/hour with a car.
C. ... you are only allowed to park on reserved places.
e:
LESSON 6 – ‘Special places’.
We did not ask 'what is right' but 'what is wrong'.
In a residential area, the maximum speed limit is 20 km/hour.

q:
When you see these two signs together, you know the speed limit only applies during the schooldays.

A. Correct.
B. Incorrect.
e:
LESSON 6 – ‘Special places’.
As long as you are in a zone, it is mandatory –e.g. the speeding limit or the parking regulations – to do what is indicated when driving into the zone, day and night.

q:
What is the maximum speed limit of a car in a bike zone?

A. Walking speed.
B. 20 km/hour.
C. 30 km/hour.
e:
LESSON 6 – ‘Special places’.
In a bike zone cars are not allowed to overtake the cyclists and speed pedelecs.
The maximum speed limit is 30 km/hour.

q: Past this sign in Flanders you are allowed to drive maximum (…) km/hour with a car.
e:
LESSON 6 – ‘Special places’.
As of 1/1/2017 in Flanders and Brussels region: 70 km/hour unless traffic signs or rules impose a different speed limit.
In Wallonia: 90 km/hour.

q:
At what maximum speed may this car drive, inside a built-up area in the Walloon Region, past this sign?

A. 30 km/h
B. 50 km/h
C. 70 km/h
e:
LESSON 6
This sign indicates, inside a built-up area, the end of a 30 km/h zone.
The car therefore continues within the built-up area.
The maximum permitted speed inside built-up areas is:
- in the Flemish Region and the Walloon Region: 50 km/h
- in the Brussels-Capital Region: 30 km/h

B. 50 km/h
C. 70 km/h

- in the Flemish Region and the Walloon Region: 50 km/h
- in the Brussels-Capital Region: 30 km/h

q:
What does this combination of signs indicate?

A. Zone where the priority of the right applies everywhere.
B. School surroundings.
e:
LESSON 6 – ‘Special places’.
The term “School surroundings” (NL Schoolomgeving - FR Abords d'école) designates an area made up of one or more public roads, or parts of a public road, including access to a school and the beginning and end of which are delimited by signals F4a and F4b. Signal A23 is associated with signal F4a.

q:
What is the maximum permitted speed within a built-up area in the Walloon Region when passing the sign indicating a pedestrian crossing?

A. Maximum 20 km/h.
B. Maximum 30 km/h.
C. Maximum 50 km/h.
e:
LESSON 6
The sign indicating a pedestrian crossing does not set a speed limit.
Within built-up areas:
- Flanders and Wallonia: maximum 50 km/h.
- Brussels Region: maximum 30 km/h.

q:
Your car is parked in this street, which suddenly becomes a school street for half an hour.
Are you allowed to leave the street with your car?

A. No, you must wait for half an hour.
B. Yes, if you drive at walking speed.
e:
LESSON 6
To reduce traffic at the start and end of the school day, a street near a school can be temporarily closed to motor vehicles. It then becomes a school street.
The closure is indicated by sign C3 (no entry) together with an additional sign stating SCHOOL STREET.
In principle, a permit is required to drive in a school street. However, leaving a parking space is allowed, provided that you drive at walking speed and proceed with extra caution.
clean_q:
Your car is parked in this street, which suddenly becomes a school street for half an hour.
Are you allowed to leave the street with your car?
A. No, you must wait for half an hour.
B. Yes, if you drive at walking speed.
clean_e:
LESSON 6
To reduce traffic at the start and end of the school day, a street near a school can be temporarily closed to motor vehicles. It then becomes a school street.
The closure is indicated by sign C3 (no entry) together with an additional sign stating SCHOOL STREET.
In principle, a permit is required to drive in a school street. However, leaving a parking space is allowed, provided that you drive at walking speed and proceed with extra caution.

What is correct?
In a residential area ...

A. ... is the maximum speed limit 25 km/hour.
B. ... are cars allowed to park on the left and right of the direction of travel.
C. ... may pedestrians and cyclists use the full lane.

LESSON 6 – ‘Special places’.
The maximum speed limit is 20 km/hour.
Only pedestrians may use the full road.

q:
You have a permit to drive your car in a play street.
A child wants to cross the road at a place without a pedestrian crossing.
Who has the right of way?

A. The child.
B. The car.

LESSON 6
In a play street, children may use the entire roadway.
Anyone allowed to drive there must proceed at walking speed.

q:
Within a built-up area in the Brussels-Capital Region, you must ...

A. ... drive at least 30 kilometers per hour.
B. ... drive at least 50 kilometers per hour.
C. ... adapt your speed to the conditions.
e:
LESSON 6 – ‘Special places’.
The maximum permitted speed within a built-up area is 30 km/h. But you are not obliged to drive 30 km/h. Often you will not be able to achieve that speed.
